headline: Manning's arm show leaves Arkansas searching for answers
body: Arch Manning put on a passing clinic that made Arkansas's secondary look like it was playing a different sport entirely. Five touchdown passes in a single outing will do that. Manning's 314 yards on 18 completions showed a quarterback who understood exactly what the Razorback coverage was doing before the snap, then punished them for it. The efficiency wasn't an accident—Texas's coaching room had clearly identified the weaknesses in Arkansas's pass defense heading into the weekend, and Manning executed the game plan with surgical precision.

What made this more damaging was how Texas's ground game softened Arkansas up first. Running back Gibson carried 15 times for 84 yards, nothing spectacular on its face, but enough to keep the Razorback defense honest and set up the play action that freed up the receivers downfield. Wide receiver Livingstone's 73-yard haul on two receptions exemplified how open Texas's pass catchers became once that run game got rolling. Arkansas's defensive line never generated consistent pressure, and when you can't get after the quarterback, talented receivers will make you pay.

Arkansas's quarterback Jackson managed just 57 passing yards on 10 completions, a statistic that tells the whole story of this game. Running back Russell scraped together 65 yards on six carries, and the Razorbacks' entire offensive identity never materialized. The Texas defense, led by Moore's two sacks, spent the afternoon in Arkansas's backfield. This wasn't a competitive game after the opening kickoff—it was a clinic on what happens when one team outprepareds and outexecutes the other.
turningPoint: Texas's first-quarter touchdown drive using play action off successful runs established the pattern that would define the entire afternoon: a defense focused on the run game leaving receivers wide open for Manning to exploit.

unsungHero.name: Gibson
unsungHero.why: His 15 carries set the foundation for everything that happened in the passing game, keeping Arkansas's linebackers from hanging over routes and preventing the secondary from playing two-high coverage
